lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That you have wrong’d me doth appear in this:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You have condemn’d and noted Lucius Pella

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For taking bribes here of the Sardians;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Wherein my letters, praying on his side,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Because I knew the man, were slighted off.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You wrong’d yourself to write in such a case.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In such a time as this it is not meet

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That every nice offence should bear his comment.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let me tell you, Cassius, you yourself

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Are much condemn’d to have an itching palm;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To sell and mart your offices for gold

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To undeservers.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I an itching palm!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You know that you are Brutus that speak this,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Or, by the gods, this speech were else your last.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The name of Cassius honours this corruption,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And chastisement doth therefore hide his head.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Chastisement!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Remember March, the ides of March remember:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Did not great Julius bleed for justice’ sake?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What villain touch’d his body, that did stab,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And not for justice? What, shall one of us,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That struck the foremost man of all this world

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But for supporting robbers, shall we now

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Contaminate our fingers with base bribes,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And sell the mighty space of our large honours

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For so much trash as may be grasped thus?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I had rather be a dog, and bay the moon,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Than such a Roman.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus, bay not me;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I’ll not endure it: you forget yourself,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To hedge me in; I am a soldier, I,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Older in practice, abler than yourself

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To make conditions.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Go to; you are not, Cassius.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I am.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I say you are not.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Urge me no more, I shall forget myself;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have mind upon your health, tempt me no further.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Away, slight man!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Is’t possible?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hear me, for I will speak.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Must I give way and room to your rash choler?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Shall I be frighted when a madman stares?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O ye gods, ye gods! must I endure all this?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: All this! ay, more: fret till your proud heart break;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Go show your slaves how choleric you are,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And make your bondmen tremble. Must I budge?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Must I observe you? must I stand and crouch

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Under your testy humour? By the gods,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You shall digest the venom of your spleen,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Though it do split you; for, from this day forth,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I’ll use you for my mirth, yea, for my laughter,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When you are waspish.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Is it come to this?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You say you are a better soldier:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let it appear so; make your vaunting true,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And it shall please me well: for mine own part,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I shall be glad to learn of noble men.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You wrong me every way; you wrong me, Brutus;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I said, an elder soldier, not a better:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Did I say “better”?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If you did, I care not.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When Caesar lived, he durst not thus have moved me.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Peace, peace! you durst not so have tempted him.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I durst not!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What, durst not tempt him!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For your life you durst not.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Do not presume too much upon my love;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I may do that I shall be sorry for.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You have done that you should be sorry for.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: There is no terror, Cassius, in your threats,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For I am arm’d so strong in honesty

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That they pass by me as the idle wind,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which I respect not. I did send to you

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For certain sums of gold, which you denied me:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For I can raise no money by vile means:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By heaven, I had rather coin my heart,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And drop my blood for drachmas, than to wring

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: From the hard hands of peasants their vile trash

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By any indirection: I did send

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To you for gold to pay my legions,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which you denied me: was that done like Cassius?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Should I have answer’d Caius Cassius so?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When Marcus Brutus grows so covetous,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To lock such rascal counters from his friends,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Be ready, gods, with all your thunderbolts;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Dash him to pieces!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I denied you not.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You did.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I did not: he was but a fool that brought

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My answer back. Brutus hath rived my heart:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A friend should bear his friend’s infirmities,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But Brutus makes mine greater than they are.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I do not, till you practise them on me.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You love me not.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I do not like your faults.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: A friendly eye could never see such faults.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: A flatterer’s would not, though they do appear

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As huge as high Olympus.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Come, Antony, and young Octavius, come,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Revenge yourselves alone on Cassius,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For Cassius is aweary of the world;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Hated by one he loves; braved by his brother;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Check’d like a bondman; all his faults observed,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Set in a note-book, learn’d, and conn’d by rote,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To cast into my teeth. O, I could weep

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My spirit from mine eyes! There is my dagger,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And here my naked breast; within, a heart

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Dearer than Plutus’ mine, richer than gold:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If that thou be’st a Roman, take it forth;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I, that denied thee gold, will give my heart:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Strike, as thou didst at Caesar; for, I know,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When thou didst hate him worst, thou lovedst him better

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Than ever thou lovedst Cassius.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Sheathe your dagger:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Be angry when you will, it shall have scope;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Do what you will, dishonour shall be humour.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O Cassius, you are yoked with a lamb

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That carries anger as the flint bears fire;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Who, much enforced, shows a hasty spark,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And straight is cold again.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Hath Cassius lived

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To be but mirth and laughter to his Brutus,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When grief, and blood ill-temper’d, vexeth him?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When I spoke that, I was ill-temper’d too.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Do you confess so much? Give me your hand.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And my heart too.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O Brutus!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What’s the matter?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Have not you love enough to bear with me,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: When that rash humour which my mother gave me

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Makes me forgetful?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Yes, Cassius; and, from henceforth,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: When you are over-earnest with your Brutus,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He’ll think your mother chides, and leave you so.

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: Let me go in to see the generals;

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: There is some grudge between ’em, ’tis not meet

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: They be alone.

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: You shall not come to them.

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: Nothing but death shall stay me.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: How now! what’s the matter?

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: For shame, you generals! what do you mean?

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: Love, and be friends, as two such men should be;

lines.speaker: POET
lines.text: For I have seen more years, I’m sure, than ye.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Ha, ha! how vilely doth this cynic rhyme!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Get you hence, sirrah; saucy fellow, hence!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Bear with him, Brutus; ’tis his fashion.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I’ll know his humour, when he knows his time:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What should the wars do with these jigging fools?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Companion, hence!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Away, away, be gone!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lucilius and Titinius, bid the commanders

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Prepare to lodge their companies to-night.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And come yourselves, and bring Messala with you

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Immediately to us.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lucius, a bowl of wine!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I did not think you could have been so angry.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O Cassius, I am sick of many griefs.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Of your philosophy you make no use,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: If you give place to accidental evils.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No man bears sorrow better. Portia is dead.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Ha! Portia!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: She is dead.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: How ’scaped I killing when I cross’d you so?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O insupportable and touching loss!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Upon what sickness?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Impatient of my absence,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And grief that young Octavius with Mark Antony

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Have made themselves so strong:—for with her death

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That tidings came;—with this she fell distract,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And, her attendants absent, swallow’d fire.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And died so?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Even so.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O ye immortal gods!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Speak no more of her. Give me a bowl of wine.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: In this I bury all unkindness, Cassius.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: My heart is thirsty for that noble pledge.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Fill, Lucius, till the wine o’erswell the cup;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I cannot drink too much of Brutus’ love.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come in, Titinius!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Welcome, good Messala.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Now sit we close about this taper here,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And call in question our necessities.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Portia, art thou gone?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No more, I pray you.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Messala, I have here received letters,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That young Octavius and Mark Antony

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come down upon us with a mighty power,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Bending their expedition toward Philippi.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Myself have letters of the self-same tenor.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: With what addition?

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: That by proscription and bills of outlawry,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Octavius, Antony, and Lepidus,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Have put to death an hundred senators.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Therein our letters do not well agree;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Mine speak of seventy senators that died

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By their proscriptions, Cicero being one.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Cicero one!

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Cicero is dead,

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: And by that order of proscription.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Had you your letters from your wife, my lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No, Messala.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Nor nothing in your letters writ of her?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Nothing, Messala.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: That, methinks, is strange.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why ask you? hear you aught of her in yours?

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: No, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Now, as you are a Roman, tell me true.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Then like a Roman bear the truth I tell:

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: For certain she is dead, and by strange manner.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why, farewell, Portia. We must die, Messala:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: With meditating that she must die once,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I have the patience to endure it now.

lines.speaker: MESSALA
lines.text: Even so great men great losses should endure.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I have as much of this in art as you,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But yet my nature could not bear it so.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Well, to our work alive. What do you think

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of marching to Philippi presently?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I do not think it good.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Your reason?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This it is:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: ’Tis better that the enemy seek us:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So shall he waste his means, weary his soldiers,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Doing himself offence; whilst we, lying still,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Are full of rest, defense, and nimbleness.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good reasons must, of force, give place to better.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The people ’twixt Philippi and this ground

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Do stand but in a forced affection;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For they have grudged us contribution:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The enemy, marching along by them,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By them shall make a fuller number up,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Come on refresh’d, new-added, and encouraged;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: From which advantage shall we cut him off,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If at Philippi we do face him there,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: These people at our back.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Hear me, good brother.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Under your pardon. You must note beside,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That we have tried the utmost of our friends,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our legions are brim-full, our cause is ripe:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The enemy increaseth every day;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: We, at the height, are ready to decline.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: There is a tide in the affairs of men,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Omitted, all the voyage of their life

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Is bound in shallows and in miseries.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: On such a full sea are we now afloat;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And we must take the current when it serves,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Or lose our ventures.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Then, with your will, go on;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: We’ll along ourselves, and meet them at Philippi.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The deep of night is crept upon our talk,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And nature must obey necessity;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Which we will niggard with a little rest.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: There is no more to say?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: No more. Good night:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Early to-morrow will we rise, and hence.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lucius! My gown. Farewell, good Messala:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good night, Titinius. Noble, noble Cassius,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good night, and good repose.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: O my dear brother!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: This was an ill beginning of the night:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Never come such division ’tween our souls!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Let it not, Brutus.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Everything is well.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Good night, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Good night, good brother.

lines.speaker: TITINIUS AND MESSALA
lines.text: Good night, Lord Brutus.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Farewell, everyone.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Give me the gown. Where is thy instrument?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Here in the tent.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What, thou speak’st drowsily?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Poor knave, I blame thee not; thou art o’erwatch’d.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Call Claudius and some other of my men;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I’ll have them sleep on cushions in my tent.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Varro and Claudius!

lines.speaker: VARRO
lines.text: Calls my lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I pray you, sirs, lie in my tent and sleep;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It may be I shall raise you by and by

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: On business to my brother Cassius.

lines.speaker: VARRO
lines.text: So please you, we will stand and watch your pleasure.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will not have it so: lie down, good sirs;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It may be I shall otherwise bethink me.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Look, Lucius, here’s the book I sought for so;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I put it in the pocket of my gown. Varro and Claudius lie down.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I was sure your lordship did not give it me.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Bear with me, good boy, I am much forgetful.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Canst thou hold up thy heavy eyes awhile,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And touch thy instrument a strain or two?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Ay, my lord, an’t please you.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It does, my boy:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I trouble thee too much, but thou art willing.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: It is my duty, sir.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I should not urge thy duty past thy might;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I know young bloods look for a time of rest.

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: I have slept, my lord, already.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It was well done; and thou shalt sleep again;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will not hold thee long: if I do live,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will be good to thee.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: This is a sleepy tune. O murderous slumber,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lay’st thou thy leaden mace upon my boy,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That plays thee music? Gentle knave, good night;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will not do thee so much wrong to wake thee:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: If thou dost nod, thou break’st thy instrument;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I’ll take it from thee; and, good boy, good night.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let me see, let me see; is not the leaf turn’d down

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Where I left reading? Here it is, I think.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: How ill this taper burns! Ha! who comes here?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I think it is the weakness of mine eyes

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That shapes this monstrous apparition.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It comes upon me. Art thou anything?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Art thou some god, some angel, or some devil,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That makest my blood cold and my hair to stare?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Speak to me what thou art.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Thy evil spirit, Brutus.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why comest thou?

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To tell thee thou shalt see me at Philippi.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Well; then I shall see thee again?

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Ay, at Philippi.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why, I will see thee at Philippi, then.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Now I have taken heart thou vanishest:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ill spirit, I would hold more talk with thee.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Boy, Lucius! Varro! Claudius! Sirs, awake!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Claudius!

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: The strings, my lord, are false.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He thinks he still is at his instrument.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Lucius, awake!

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Didst thou dream, Lucius, that thou so criedst out?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: My lord, I do not know that I did cry.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Yes, that thou didst: didst thou see anything?

lines.speaker: LUCIUS
lines.text: Nothing, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Sleep again, Lucius. Sirrah Claudius!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To Varro. Fellow thou, awake!

lines.speaker: VARRO
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: CLAUDIUS
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why did you so cry out, sirs, in your sleep?

lines.speaker: VARRO AND CLAUDIUS
lines.text: Did we, my lord?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ay: saw you anything?

lines.speaker: VARRO
lines.text: No, my lord, I saw nothing.

lines.speaker: CLAUDIUS
lines.text: Nor I, my lord.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Go and commend me to my brother Cassius;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Bid him set on his powers betimes before,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And we will follow.

lines.speaker: VARRO AND CLAUDIUS
lines.text: It shall be done, my lord.

stage_directions.text: Enter Poet, followed by Lucilius, Titinius, and Lucius.
Reenter Lucius, with wine and taper.

glosses.definition: publicly censured and marked down as guilty
a Roman ally of Cassius, convicted of corruption in the province of Sardis; the historical incident is in Plutarch
dismissed contemptuously
fitting; proper

glosses.phrase: every nice offence should bear his comment
glosses.definition: every petty offence should be publicly censured
greedy hand (itching for bribes) — proverbial; the play's most direct charge of corruption
sell and traffic in your official appointments — a specific crime of Roman political corruption
Brutus's damning rhetorical pivot — we killed Caesar FOR this kind of corruption, and you now commit it; the assassination's justification is under threat

glosses.phrase: foremost man of all this world
glosses.definition: Caesar described in superlative terms by the man who killed him — a measure of Brutus's residual honour
defending bribers and extortioners
howl at the moon (like a dog) — be futilely noisy
confine me; constrain me
older in experience; more seasoned
dismissive exclamation — 'come on,' 'get out'
take care of your own safety — Cassius's veiled threat
insignificant man — Brutus returns the insult
hasty anger (choler = yellow bile, one of the four humours; associated with irascibility)
angry (from the humoral psychology)
make your slaves tremble
irritable disposition
poison of your bile — the spleen was thought to be the seat of anger
fretful; stinging like a wasp
Cassius's climb-down — redefining his claim mid-argument

glosses.phrase: presume too much upon my love
glosses.definition: take my love for granted
fortified by my integrity
mint my own heart into coins — I would rather bleed money from my own body than extort it
shed my own blood for coinage (drachma = Greek silver coin)

glosses.phrase: wring from the hard hands of peasants
glosses.definition: extract by force from working men's calloused hands
crooked means
worthless reckoning-tokens — low money; Brutus's dismissive view of cash
split my heart (rived = cloven)
tolerate his friend's weaknesses
as large as Mount Olympus — the classical seat of the gods
rebuked like a slave
memorised by repetition
the mine of Plutus, Greek god of wealth; richer than any gold-mine

glosses.phrase: Strike, as thou didst at Caesar
glosses.definition: Cassius offers his breast as Brutus offered his dagger to Caesar — the quarrel repeats the assassination in miniature

glosses.phrase: When thou didst hate him worst, thou lovedst him better
glosses.definition: Cassius's keen wound: Brutus loved Caesar even in killing him more than he ever loved Cassius
paired in harness with a lamb — Brutus's ironic self-description as the mild partner

glosses.phrase: carries anger as the flint bears fire
glosses.definition: holds anger as flint holds fire — sparks only when struck, then cold

glosses.phrase: rash humour which my mother gave me
glosses.definition: the hot temper inherited from his mother — Cassius's half-apology via humoral inheritance
the unnamed Poet who interrupts the reconciliation — a cynical philosopher-poet in Plutarch; here played for comic relief that turns on the poet's uselessness in war
Cynic rhyme — verse in the manner of the Cynic philosophers (bare, moralising); Plutarch calls this figure 'Marcus Phaonius,' a follower of Cato who affected Cynic manners
impudent fellow

glosses.phrase: I'll know his humour, when he knows his time
glosses.definition: I'll tolerate his manner when he learns the right time — i.e. never, mid-war

glosses.phrase: What should the wars do with these jigging fools?
glosses.definition: what use have wars for these rhyme-jigging fools? — the play's dismissive question about poets in political crisis; Shakespeare's metapoetical self-awareness
fellow (dismissive, as in 'fellow!')

glosses.phrase: Of your philosophy you make no use
glosses.definition: you're not using your Stoic philosophy — Brutus was a professed Stoic; the complaint is apt

Brutus announces Portia's death; a substantive loss. The double-report problem: he repeats the news later (L183ff) as if hearing it for the first time. Some editors read this as a textual-revision artifact — Shakespeare may have cut one version and failed to remove the other
unbearable, heart-moving loss
became distraught; went mad
Portia killed herself by swallowing burning coals — the traditional, Plutarchan account of her suicide; one of the ancient world's most distinctive suicides
consider what we need (for the war)
city in Macedonia (northern Greece); site of the decisive double battle of October 42 BCE, where Antony and Octavius defeated Brutus and Cassius
the triumvirs' lists of enemies to be killed and property seized; 'bills of outlawry' = public notices of outlawing

glosses.phrase: put to death an hundred senators
glosses.definition: executed a hundred senators — Messala's news of the triumvirate's political purge; Plutarch gives 130; it was a systematic dismantling of the senatorial class
Cicero was among those killed in the proscriptions of 43 BCE — Antony, whom Cicero had attacked in the *Philippics*, put him on the list
Marcus Tullius Cicero, Roman orator, philosopher, and statesman, executed December 7, 43 BCE — the death Messala confirms

glosses.phrase: like a Roman bear the truth
glosses.definition: endure the truth as a Roman should — Stoic fortitude invoked

glosses.phrase: I have as much of this in art as you
glosses.definition: my philosophy (art = learned discipline) teaches me the same lesson — Cassius acknowledges Stoicism but admits his nature can't bear it
to the living business at hand

glosses.phrase: Good reasons must, of force, give place to better
glosses.definition: good reasons must yield to better ones

glosses.phrase: tide in the affairs of men
glosses.definition: a tide in human affairs — Brutus's most-quoted line; a maritime metaphor for opportunity
caught at high water; seized at the right moment

glosses.phrase: bound in shallows and in miseries
glosses.definition: stuck in shallow waters and troubles — the opportunity missed
high tide — the moment for action
be stingy with a brief sleep — take only a little
exhausted from over-long watching

Brutus is reading a book — with turning pages and a bookmark leaf ('is not the leaf turn'd down'). Roman reading was on scrolls; the codex (book with pages) wasn't common until the 2nd century CE. Another deliberate anachronism that places Rome in an English study
sleep personified as a murderer
a heavy lead mace — the weapon of Sleep in Renaissance allegory
a page folded over as a bookmark — anachronism confirmed; scrolls don't have leaves to turn down
terrible supernatural appearance — Caesar's Ghost
your evil genius — in Roman belief, each person had a guiding spirit (genius); the ghost announces itself as Brutus's shadow-self, not specifically as Caesar. The ambiguity is Shakespeare's
the ghost's prophecy — he will appear again at Philippi, where Brutus dies
Lucius's sleep-talking about his instrument — the strings have gone out of tune; a touching domestic detail amid the supernatural
set his forces marching early

annotations.title: The quarrel as relationship study — Shakespeare's intimate scene between men
annotations.body:
The Brutus-Cassius quarrel is among Shakespeare's most carefully observed portraits of male political friendship. It is not a disagreement about tactics that happens to become heated; it is a heated scene that happens to surface tactical disagreements. What is primarily being transacted is the relationship itself.

The quarrel moves through distinct registers: the professional grievance (Lucius Pella's corruption), the moral grievance (Cassius's 'itching palm'), the personal wound (Cassius's complaint that Brutus makes his infirmities larger than they are), the theatrical threat (Cassius offers his dagger as Brutus offered his to Caesar), and the reconciliation ('When I spoke that, I was ill-temper'd too'). Shakespeare writes male intimacy here with a specificity he rarely grants it elsewhere. The stage direction 'Give me your hand' (L114) and Brutus's 'And my heart too' (L115) are among the frankest gestures of affection between men in the canon.

And the scene also enacts what Brutus saw in 4.2: Cassius under pressure — 'bloody spur' — fails his vaunt. His argument for marching on Philippi (5.1) will, tactically, have been correct; Brutus's will prove wrong. But Cassius has already ceded the moral ground. Brutus's willingness to quarrel is leverage; Cassius's willingness to be hurt is defeat. The play's insistent question of who leads has been answered in this scene, and the answer is catastrophic.

Plutarch's *Life of Brutus* §34 records the quarrel but not the emotional specificity. The dagger moment and the 'Give me your hand / And my heart too' exchange are Shakespeare's invention. They make the scene not military-political debate but a study of love under threat.
sources: Bruce R. Smith, *Homosexual Desire in Shakespeare's England* (1991)

annotations.title: 'Itching palm': the conspiracy's moral collapse, in one accusation
annotations.body:
Brutus accuses Cassius of taking bribes — 'to sell and mart your offices for gold / To undeservers.' The specificity is important: this is not generic greed but the sale of political offices, one of the defined crimes of Roman corruption. Brutus links the charge, with devastating aim, to the assassination itself: 'Remember March, the ides of March remember: / Did not great Julius bleed for justice' sake? / What villain touch'd his body, that did stab, / And not for justice?'

The argument reads the ides retrospectively: we killed Caesar to prevent *this kind of corruption*. If we then commit it, the assassination was not the purification Brutus claimed it was; it was just a change of personnel. The whole moral case for the murder depends, in Brutus's reading, on the conspirators' subsequent conduct — and Cassius's conduct has failed.

Cassius does not deny the bribery. He threatens Brutus. Shakespeare is implying, without stating, that the accusation lands because it is true. The conspiracy has begun to rot into the thing it killed Caesar to prevent. When Brutus himself later says 'I can raise no money by vile means' (L69) and admits that he sent to Cassius for money, the scene has laid the full case: the man of principle is dependent on the man of corruption, because principle alone does not pay armies.

annotations.title: The Poet interrupts — Shakespeare on poetry's place in war
annotations.body:
Immediately after the reconciliation, a Poet forces his way past Lucilius into the tent: 'For shame, you generals! what do you mean? / Love, and be friends.' Brutus and Cassius dismiss him together — Cassius with 'Ha, ha! how vilely doth this cynic rhyme!' and Brutus with the savage 'What should the wars do with these jigging fools?'

The scene is one of two in the play (with 3.3) in which a poet appears. Both poets are bad poets; both poets are killed or thrown out. Shakespeare, writing 'jigging fools' into the mouth of a tragic hero, has a specific target — the jig, an irreverent verse-and-dance that often followed or interrupted Elizabethan tragedies; a form that Shakespeare's company had reduced but not eliminated. The Poet is Cinna-adjacent: an irritant to the serious political business, a reminder that poetry is outside political power.

Yet the Poet has just, crudely, said something correct. Brutus and Cassius should love and be friends. The intrusion of crude rhyme onto refined political relationship is Shakespeare's mild self-mockery: the play itself is a poem, inserted into politics, and its claim to be taken seriously rests on refusing the Poet's rhyme while making its own version of his point. 'What should the wars do with these jigging fools?' is the play's self-critique, spoken from inside itself.
sources: David Bevington, *This Wide and Universal Theater* (2007)

annotations.title: Portia's double death: an editorial crux
annotations.body:
Brutus announces Portia's death at L143 ('Portia is dead'). Cassius responds with grief; the two drink. Then Messala arrives with news of the proscriptions (L167ff), and at L181 Brutus asks Messala, 'Hear you aught of her in yours?' and at L183–185 hears again that Portia is dead — and reacts as if it were news.

The duplication has troubled editors since the 18th century. The most common scholarly reading (going back to George Steevens in 1778) is that the passage is a revision artifact: Shakespeare wrote one version of the announcement (the private, emotional one with Cassius), then wrote another (the public, Stoic one with Messala), and failed to remove the first when adapting. Alternatively, Brutus is performing Roman fortitude for Messala's benefit, demonstrating the Stoic mastery the news requires. Both readings have defenders; neither is certain.

What is certain is that the second report is Brutus's most explicit Stoic performance: 'Why, farewell, Portia. We must die, Messala: / With meditating that she must die once, / I have the patience to endure it now.' Messala's response — 'Even so great men great losses should endure' — is approval of the performance, not of the emotion. The scene is made double-uncanny by being doubled: in the private register Brutus is grief-struck; in the public register he is a Stoic monument. Shakespeare may have meant both, may have revised incompletely, may have left the textual strangeness as a feature. Most modern productions play only one of the two, cutting the other.
sources: George Steevens (ed.), Shakespeare (1778) — first scholarly note on the duplication
David Daniell (ed.), Arden 3 (1998), Appendix on textual history
Fredson Bowers, 'The Copy for Shakespeare's *Julius Caesar*', *SN&Q* 26 (1976)
John Dover Wilson, *The New Shakespeare: Julius Caesar* (1949)

annotations.title: 'There is a tide in the affairs of men' — Brutus loses the argument
annotations.body:
Brutus's most-quoted speech is also his third catastrophic tactical error. Cassius's position — wait for Antony and Octavius to come to them, let the enemy exhaust himself in march — is military orthodoxy. Brutus's position, that the 'tide' favours an immediate move on Philippi, is lyrical, aphoristic, and wrong. The speech wins the argument on rhetorical force. Brutus has absorbed Cassius's 1.2 lesson ('Men at some time are masters of their fates') and turned it back at Cassius, now wielding the agency-language to argue for action. Cassius yields.

The irony is exact. Cassius was right in 1.2 to argue for agency; Brutus was right in 2.1 to join the plot; the two men's successive exercises of agency have brought them here, and now Brutus's insistence on acting once more delivers them to Philippi, where Cassius will kill himself on his own sword and Brutus will fall on Strato's. The tide metaphor is consistent with Brutus's entire philosophy — that the moment of decision matters — and consistent also with his record, which is that every decision he has made on that philosophy has lost.

The speech is taught and quoted outside its context; inside it, it is the sound of a man talking his friend into a grave.

annotations.title: Brutus's book and the Ghost: anachronism and theatre
annotations.body:
Two details of the tent scene work in parallel. First, Brutus is reading a book — 'the leaf turn'd down / Where I left reading.' Second, the Ghost of Caesar appears to him. Both are Shakespeare's inventions against the facts.

The book is another deliberate anachronism. Roman reading was on scrolls, not codices with turning pages. The codex — a bound book with leaves — did not become common until the 2nd century CE. Brutus 'turning down a leaf' is an Elizabethan gentleman's reading practice, transposed. The anachronism works the way the clock in 2.1 works: it makes Rome legible as a place of English intellectual life. Brutus is thus one of Shakespeare's small library of reading heroes — with Hamlet and Prospero — figures defined partly by their relation to a codex.

The Ghost is more ambiguous. 'Thy evil spirit, Brutus,' it says — not 'Caesar's revenge,' not a specific person, but an emanation of Brutus himself. Plutarch's *Life of Brutus* §36 reports the ghost as a specific 'strange and monstrous shape' but does not name it; 'Brutus asked what it was, a man, or a god... The evil spirit replied, I am thy evil angel, Brutus, and thou shalt see me by the city of Philippi.' Shakespeare follows Plutarch's language closely.

The ambiguity is specific. The Ghost *is* Caesar in the stage tradition (he returns in 5.5 as 'the ghost of Caesar'), and it *is* Brutus's 'evil spirit' in its self-naming. Shakespeare is allowing both: Caesar's revenge and Brutus's haunted conscience are not two things here but one thing. The play's metaphysics insist on the identity.

options: A relationship study — the scene is about love between two men under threat; tactical content is subordinate to emotional register; the 'Give me your hand / And my heart too' exchange is among the frankest gestures of male affection in Shakespeare

trials.prompt: Brutus's 'itching palm' accusation and Cassius's non-denial imply:
options: The charge lands because it is true; the conspiracy has begun to rot into the corruption it killed Caesar to prevent, and Brutus's 'remember the ides of March' frames the accusation as retrospective moral reckoning on the whole assassination

trials.prompt: 'What should the wars do with these jigging fools?' is Shakespeare's:
options: Wry self-reference — 'jig' was a specific Elizabethan comic form that sometimes followed tragedy; the play, a poem about politics, mocks crude poems about politics while quietly making its own

trials.prompt: Why does Brutus seem to learn of Portia's death twice (L143 and L183)?
options: A long-recognised textual crux: likely a revision artifact where Shakespeare wrote the news in both a private (emotional) and a public (Stoic) register and didn't remove one; alternatively, Brutus performs Stoic fortitude for Messala's benefit

trials.prompt: 'There is a tide in the affairs of men' is famous outside its context. Inside the play, it is:
options: The rhetorical force by which Brutus wins the argument to march on Philippi over Cassius's militarily-correct objection — a third catastrophic decision after sparing Antony (2.1) and letting Antony speak (3.1)

trials.prompt: What is anachronistic about Brutus reading a book in the tent?
options: Roman reading was on scrolls; the codex (bound book with leaves to turn) didn't become common until the 2nd century CE — another Renaissance-in-Rome anachronism, placing Brutus among Shakespeare's intellectual-hero readers (Hamlet, Prospero)
The book was written in Greek

trials.prompt: The Ghost announces itself as 'Thy evil spirit, Brutus' — what is ambiguous about this?
options: The Ghost is both Caesar (it returns in 5.5 as Caesar's ghost) and Brutus's own evil angel (Plutarch's language); Shakespeare preserves the ambiguity, making Caesar's revenge and Brutus's haunted conscience a single phenomenon
Only that its voice is loud

trials.prompt: After the quarrel and reconciliation, what does Cassius concede by asking 'Hath Cassius lived / To be but mirth and laughter to his Brutus'?
options: That the relationship has turned — Brutus holds the moral position, Cassius the dependent one; the man who seduced Brutus in 1.2 now pleads for warmth
That he is abandoning the alliance
